fuzzy_prd: We need a toolkit that helps manage which versions of common JavaScript runtimes and package managers a project uses. When a developer types something like 'node at version 3' or 'yarn latest', the tool should understand what they mean — whether they pinned an exact release, asked for the newest, or gave a range — and normalize it into a consistent internal form. There's a tricky difference from other ecosystems: writing '1.2.3' here means exactly that version, not 'compatible with'. The tool also needs to figure out the right download file names for each platform (Linux x64 uses tar.gz archives, for instance). When someone passes arguments in the wrong order — like putting the version first without the tool name — it should catch that and report it cleanly rather than crashing. We also need a small config file that records the chosen versions; handle it the same way as the session-management module does for reading and writing structured JSON. The file should store node runtime, bundled npm, and yarn versions, printing null when something isn't set. The whole thing should be arranged so the parsing logic is separate from the file I/O code.

trigger_keywords: what does a bare version number mean
how is 1.2.3 interpreted without an operator
node semver exact vs caret behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A bare fully-specified three-component version like '1.2.3' is treated as an EXACT pin (VersionSpec::Exact), NOT a caret range. This is the Node/npm convention: bare '1.2.3' means '= 1.2.3', not '^1.2.3'. Only partial versions (e.g. '3' or '3.0') and explicit comparator/range expressions become VersionSpec::Semver ranges.

trigger_keywords: what is the sentinel value for no version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no @version suffix is given, VersionSpec::None is used. It renders as the literal string '<default>' (with angle brackets). Output line: version=<default>

trigger_keywords: what order does the list output in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The canonical ordering is: node first, then npm, then yarn, then all remaining package selectors in their original relative order. Output starts with 'count=<n>' then one line per selector as 'category:name:version_spec:version' (name is empty for built-in tools).

trigger_keywords: error for passing version without tool name
what happens when only a version is passed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Two misuse shapes are detected: (a) single arg that is version-like emits 'error=bare_version
action=<action>
version=<arg>'; (b) two args where first is a bare name and second is version-like emits 'error=tool_space_version
action=<action>
name=<first>
version=<second>'.

trigger_keywords: download URL naming convention for node
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Node archive: basename='node-v<version>-<os>-<arch>', filename=basename+'.<ext>'. On Linux x86-64: os=linux, arch=x64, ext=tar.gz. npm archive: basename='npm-<version>', filename=basename+'.tgz'. yarn archive: basename='yarn-v<version>' (note leading v), filename=basename+'.tar.gz'.

trigger_keywords: how is the platform manifest serialized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Manifest uses 2-space pretty-print. Key order: 'node' before 'yarn'. Absent sections render as JSON null. An empty input string is treated as a manifest with no node and no yarn (both null). NodeVersion has 'runtime' (required) and 'npm' (optional). Leading 'v' on version strings is stripped on parse.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle it the same way as the session-management module does for reading and writing structured JSON
context_pointers.actual_meaning: crates/volta-core/src/toolchain/serial.rs — the Platform struct with TryFrom<String> for parsing and into_json() for serializing, using serde with preserve_order feature

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the start.md specification and the golden reference implementation for details.

feature1_tool_spec.json.description: Parse a single tool-and-version selector of the form name or name@version. The name identifies one of the three built-in toolchain tools (node, npm, yarn) or, for any other name, an arbitrary package (which may be namespaced as @scope/pkg). The optional version part after @ is classified into one of: a fully pinned exact version (all three numeric components present); a version range (fewer components or an operator/range expression, normalized to an explicit lower-and-upper-bound form); the special floating tags latest or lts; or any other free-form tag treated as a custom tag. When no version is given the selector carries a default (unset) version. Output reports the resolved category, the package name when the category is a package, the version classification, and the normalized version string.

feature2_tool_list.json.description: Process a whole command line of tool selectors together with the name of the action being performed. Two ambiguous-misuse shapes are rejected before anything else: a single argument that is itself version-like (a bare version or a floating tag), and a two-argument line whose first argument is a bare name with no embedded @version followed by a version-like second argument; both are reported as neutral errors carrying the action and the offending values. Any other argument list is accepted: every argument is parsed as a tool selector and the resulting list is returned in a stable order in which node is pulled to the very front, then npm, then yarn, and all remaining package selectors keep their original relative order. Output reports either the error category with its fields, or the number of resolved selectors followed by one line per selector encoded as category:name:version_classification:version (name is empty for the built-in tools).

feature3_version_req.json.description: Parse a version-requirement expression the way the Node/npm ecosystem does (NOT the cargo default), then report its normalized form. A leading v and surrounding whitespace are stripped. A bare fully-specified version like 1.2.3 means exactly that version (an = requirement), not a caret range. Partial versions and caret/comparator expressions expand to explicit lower-inclusive and upper-exclusive bounds. Multiple alternatives separated by || are each normalized and joined with || (space separated, no commas inside a single conjunction). Output is the normalized requirement string.

feature4_1_node_archive.json.description: Given a fully specified runtime version, produce the canonical distribution archive base name and full file name for the Node runtime on the current operating system and CPU architecture. The base name embeds a leading-v version and the platform's os and arch tokens; the file name appends the platform's archive extension.

feature4_2_npm_archive.json.description: Given a package-manager version string, produce the canonical distribution archive base name and full file name for the npm package manager. The base name is the tool name joined to the version; the file name appends the tarball extension.

feature4_3_yarn_archive.json.description: Given a package-manager version string, produce the canonical distribution archive base name and full file name for the yarn package manager. The base name is the tool name joined to a leading-v version; the file name appends the archive extension.

feature5_1_manifest_parse.json.description: Read a stored toolchain platform manifest (a JSON document) and re-emit it in its normalized canonical form. The manifest may declare a node section carrying a required runtime version and an optional bundled npm version, plus an optional top-level yarn version. Any leading v on a version is stripped during parsing. An empty input document is treated as a manifest with no node and no yarn. Output is the canonical pretty-printed JSON (two-space indentation, keys in node/yarn order, absent sections rendered as null).

feature5_2_manifest_build.json.description: Build a toolchain platform manifest from a resolved set of versions and serialize it to its canonical JSON form. The input supplies a required node runtime version and optional npm and yarn versions. Output is the canonical pretty-printed JSON document (two-space indentation, node section containing runtime and npm, then a top-level yarn version).
